phase_detection.complete: (function(){return !!(document.body && document.body.textContent && document.body.textContent.indexOf('Thank you for your participation') !== -1);})()
phase_detection.instructions: (function(){var el=document.querySelector('#jspsych-content'); var t=el?(el.textContent||''):''; return !!document.querySelector('#jspsych-instructions-next, #jspsych-fullscreen-btn') || t.indexOf('Press space to begin')!==-1 || t.indexOf('Get ready')!==-1;})()
phase_detection.feedback: (function(){var el=document.querySelector('#jspsych-content'); if(!el) return false; var t=el.textContent||''; return t.indexOf('Average response time')!==-1 || t.indexOf('Proportion missed go')!==-1 || t.indexOf('Proportion correct stops')!==-1 || t.indexOf('correct response')!==-1 || t.indexOf('incorrect response')!==-1 || t.indexOf('correct stop')!==-1 || t.indexOf('remember: try to stop')!==-1 || t.indexOf('too slow')!==-1 || t.indexOf('too fast')!==-1;})()

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P., Beste, C., Bissett, P. G., Brockett, A. T., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Mean go RT in healthy young adults performing the standard STOP-IT keyboard arrow task is typically in the 500–600 ms range.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Across two-choice tasks, ex-Gaussian mu typically falls between ~380 and ~480 ms with sigma 40–80 ms and tau 80–150 ms.

citations.authors: Lacouture, Y., & Cousineau, D.
citations.title: How to use MATLAB to fit the ex-Gaussian and other probability functions to a distribution of response times
citations.quote: Typical estimates for the ex-Gaussian on choice RT yield mu in the 400–450 ms range for healthy adults.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Sigma estimates from choice-RT tasks generally lie between 40 and 80 ms in unimpaired adults.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Sigma values were generally in the 30–70 ms range across conditions.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Tau, the exponential component, captures slow attentional lapses and ranges from roughly 70 to 160 ms across studies.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Brown, S., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Quantile maximum likelihood estimation of response time distributions
citations.quote: Tau is generally larger than sigma and reflects late-tail slowing of cognitive responses.

go_left.rationale: Healthy-adult mean go RT in keyboard arrow STOP-IT averages ~530ms (Verbruggen et al. 2019; Vermeylen & Verbruggen 2019 norms). Ex-Gaussian decomposition of choice RT distributions yields mu ~400-440ms (Gaussian mode reflecting decision + motor latency), sigma ~50-70ms (within-trial perceptual/motor noise), and tau ~90-130ms (exponential tail from attentional lapses). MAXRT=1250ms is far above the tail so no truncation bias.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Two-choice arrow discrimination shows no reliable left/right asymmetry in mean go RT for right-handed adults using ArrowLeft/ArrowRight responses.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters
citations.quote: Mu in two-choice tasks ranges 380–480 ms; left vs right response hands rarely differ by more than ~10 ms.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters
citations.quote: Sigma typically 40–80 ms; symmetric for left vs right key responses.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters
citations.quote: Tau ~70–160 ms across choice-RT studies; not modulated by response hand.

go_right.rationale: Two-choice arrow discrimination is left/right symmetric for right-handed adults using ArrowLeft/ArrowRight; published STOP-IT norms show no reliable asymmetry. Parameters mirror go_left.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., & Cowan, W. B.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: Signal-respond RTs are systematically faster than mean go RT because they represent the subset of go responses that escaped the stop process.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Failed-inhibition (signal-respond) RTs are typically 30–80 ms shorter than mean go RT, consistent with the independent race model.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions
citations.quote: When SSD is staircased to p(respond|signal)=0.5, signal-respond RT approximates the median of the lower half of the go RT distribution.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., & Cowan, W. B.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action
citations.quote: Signal-respond RT distributions are a left-truncated subset of the go distribution, yielding slightly reduced sigma.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: The signal-respond distribution is narrower than the full go distribution because the slowest go responses are inhibited.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., & Cowan, W. B.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action
citations.quote: The slow tail of the go distribution is preferentially inhibited; signal-respond RT therefore exhibits a compressed exponential component.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Tau on signal-respond trials is reduced relative to go-trial tau because slow attentional-lapse trials are most likely to be inhibited.

stop_signal.rationale: Per the independent race model (Logan & Cowan 1984), signal-respond RTs on failed-inhibition trials are systematically ~50-80ms faster than mean go RT because the go process must finish before the (delayed) stop process. With adaptive SSD staircase converging on p(respond|signal)=0.5, the failed-stop RT distribution is the faster portion of the go distribution: mu ~360, sigma ~55, tau ~90. This condition aggregates both correctly-inhibited (no RT) and failed-inhibition outcomes; the value parameters describe the failed-inhibition subset.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Lag-1 autocorrelation of choice-RT series is typically positive and small, in the range of 0.05–0.25.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Empirical lag-1 autocorrelations across choice-RT datasets ranged from approximately 0.08 to 0.22.

autocorrelation.rationale: Lag-1 autocorrelation phi ~0.10-0.20 in choice-RT series reflects slow fluctuations in attention/arousal (Gilden 2001; Wagenmakers et al. 2004). Documented in stop-signal go-RT series.

condition_repetition.rationale: Repetition priming for arrow direction is minimal in two-choice keyboard tasks and is not a documented signature of the stop-signal paradigm. Enabling could bias context-independence checks of the race model.

citations.authors: Lim, J., & Dinges, D. F.
citations.title: Sleep deprivation and vigilant attention
citations.quote: Time-on-task within a 10-15 minute simple/choice RT block produces ~10-30 ms of mean RT slowing across the run.

citations.authors: Massar, S. A. A., Lim, J., Sasmita, K., & Chee, M. W. L.
citations.title: Sleep deprivation, effort allocation and performance
citations.quote: Vigilance decrement on speeded tasks accumulates at roughly 0.03–0.10 ms per trial in well-rested adults across a single 10–20 minute block.

fatigue_drift.rationale: Modest on-task slowing of ~10-20ms across the 256-experimental-trial run is reported in STOP-IT block-level summaries. drift_per_trial_ms=0.05 yields ~13ms across 256 trials, consistent with norms for short (~15min) sessions.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Long-range fluctuations in choice-RT series typically contribute 15–40 ms of slow drift beyond white noise.

citations.authors: Van Orden, G. C., Holden, J. G., & Turvey, M. T.
citations.title: Self-organization of cognitive performance
citations.quote: Slow 1/f-like RT fluctuations in choice tasks have characteristic amplitudes on the order of tens of milliseconds.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: RT series in choice tasks consistently exhibit Hurst exponents in the 0.7–0.9 range, indicative of long-range positive dependence.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Estimated Hurst exponents typically range from approximately 0.65 to 0.85 in choice-RT datasets.

pink_noise.rationale: 1/f^alpha (pink) noise with Hurst ~0.7-0.8 is a robust feature of choice-RT series including stop-signal (Gilden 2001; Van Orden et al. 2003). sd_ms=25 captures slow drift beyond white noise without inflating short-lag variance.

citations.authors: Rabbitt, P. M. A.
citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.quote: Subjects systematically slowed their responses on the trial following an error.

citations.authors: Dutilh, G., van Ravenzwaaij, D., Nieuwenhuis, S., van der Maas, H. L. J., Forstmann, B. U., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: How to measure post-error slowing: A confound and a simple solution
citations.quote: Post-error slowing magnitudes from choice-RT tasks typically lie between 10 and 50 ms.

citations.authors: Dutilh, G., van Ravenzwaaij, D., Nieuwenhuis, S., van der Maas, H. L. J., Forstmann, B. U., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: How to measure post-error slowing: A confound and a simple solution
citations.quote: Post-error slowing of 30–60 ms is commonly reported across choice-RT and stop-signal tasks.

citations.authors: Schachar, R. J., Chen, S., Logan, G. D., et al.
citations.title: Evidence for an error monitoring deficit in attention deficit hyperactivity disorder
citations.quote: Healthy controls slowed responses by approximately 40 ms following stop-signal failures and choice errors.

post_error_slowing.rationale: Post-error slowing of 20-40ms after choice errors is well-replicated (Rabbitt 1966; Dutilh et al. 2012; Schachar et al. 2004 in stop-signal). Applies after go-trial choice errors and after failed-stop trials.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Logan, G. D., Liefooghe, B., & Vandierendonck, A.
citations.title: Short-term aftereffects of response inhibition: Repetition priming or between-trial control adjustments?
citations.quote: Mean go RT was reliably slower on trials immediately following a stop-signal trial, with effect sizes around 20–40 ms.

citations.authors: Bissett, P. G., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Balancing cognitive demand: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Go RTs increased by ~20–60 ms following stop-signal trials, indicating proactive control adjustments.

citations.authors: Bissett, P. G., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Balancing cognitive demand: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Post-stop slowing reached approximately 50–70 ms in blocks with higher stop-signal probability.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Logan, G. D., Liefooghe, B., & Vandierendonck, A.
citations.title: Short-term aftereffects of response inhibition
citations.quote: Aftereffect of stop-signal trials on subsequent go RT was on the order of 30–60 ms.

post_interrupt_slowing.rationale: Go-RT slowing of 20-60ms following stop-signal trials (regardless of inhibition outcome) is robustly documented (Verbruggen et al. 2008; Bissett & Logan 2011). Reflects strategic/proactive caution and is the canonical violation of race-model context-independence in stop-signal.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Between-subject SD of mean go RT in healthy adults on STOP-IT is ~80-100ms (Vermeylen & Verbruggen 2019; large web norms). Within-subject condition-level SD ~30-40ms reflects individual differences in choice-RT processing speed. sigma_tau_range [0.7, 1.4] scales the Gaussian/exponential components per individual to capture variability in distribution shape (Heathcote et al. 1991, 2004). Two-choice arrow accuracy SD ~0.02-0.03 across adults; omission SD ~0.005 given the relatively forgiving 1250ms response window.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of mean go RT in healthy young-adult samples is typically ~80–110 ms.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Between-subject SDs of mean RT for two-choice and stop-signal tasks ranged ~70–110 ms in adult samples.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Within-subject condition-level RT SDs in choice and stop-signal tasks were typically 25–45 ms across adults.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions
citations.quote: Across-subject variation in condition-specific RT in STOP-IT is on the order of 30–40 ms.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Individual ex-Gaussian sigma and tau estimates varied roughly 2-fold across participants within a condition.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters
citations.quote: Across studies, individual sigma and tau parameters span roughly 0.6×–1.5× the group mean.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors
citations.quote: Two-choice accuracy on go trials in healthy adults averages ~95–98% with between-subject SD ~0.02–0.04.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox
citations.quote: Between-subject SDs of choice accuracy in adult speeded tasks were typically 0.02–0.03.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions
citations.quote: Go-trial omissions in adult STOP-IT are rare (mean ~0.5–2%) with between-subject SD on the order of 0.005–0.015.

reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'stop_signal_kywch_jspsych' with 4 stimuli. Source files: jquery-1.7.1.min.js, jspsych.js, jspsych-instructions.js, jspsych-fullscreen.js, jspsych-call-function.js, jspsych-html-keyboard-response.js, jspsych-detect-held-down-keys.js, custom-stop-signal-plugi.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 3 conditions; enabled 5 temporal effects.
Produced 43 citations across 22 numeric parameters with literature_range and between_subject_sd.
Tagged sensitivity on 28 parameters; 9 are high-sensitivity.
